title: Zero-Shot Classification
concept: Zero-shot classification allows CLIP to categorize images by comparing their embeddings to text prompt embeddings representing classes, without task-specific training.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What makes up the two branches of CLIP's dual encoder?

options.text: An image encoder (ViT/CNN) and a text encoder (Transformer).
A convolutional backbone and an MLP head.
An encoder and a decoder stack.

exercises.explain: Correct! CLIP maps pixels and words using two separate encoders.

cards.title: No training required
cards.body: If you want a standard model to classify dog breeds, you must gather hundreds of labeled breeding photos and train a new classifier head. CLIP can classify them immediately, without seeing a single training image for this task! Let's explore the magic of zero-shot classification.

cards.title: Zero-Shot Classification
cards.body: Zero-shot classification is the ability to categorize images into arbitrary classes without retraining. We write class labels as text prompts (e.g. 'a photo of a [class]'), generate their text embeddings, and select the class whose text vector is **closest** to the input image vector.

cards.title: Label matching
cards.body: Imagine you have an unlabeled photo of a tropical bird. You are handed three stickers: 'a photo of a seagull', 'a photo of a parrot', and 'a photo of a penguin'. You read the stickers and stick the one that describes the photo best. You didn't need to train a model; you just matched description to image.

cards.title: Prompt templates
cards.body: Instead of passing raw words like 'dog', we get better results using templates like 'a photo of a dog, a type of pet'. This matches the full-sentence context CLIP saw during pre-training.

cards.title: Not always perfect
cards.body: You might think CLIP can classify anything. While excellent at general categories, CLIP struggles with **fine-grained tasks** (like counting objects or distinguishing between highly similar industrial parts) compared to custom-trained models.

left.text:
Add new head -> Collect labels
Re-train weights with loss
Locked into set training classes

right.text:
Feed class text prompts
Compare vector distances
Flexible: change classes instantly

exercises.prompt: How does CLIP perform zero-shot classification?
exercises.explain: CLIP finds the class prompt vector that shares the highest cosine similarity with the input image vector.

options.text: By comparing the image embedding vector to text embedding vectors of candidate class prompts
By retraining its image encoder on validation labels
By converting the input image to a text document directly
By ignoring the image encoder during prediction

exercises.prompt: True or false: CLIP requires retraining its weights whenever you want to add a new category.
exercises.explain: False. You can add new category prompts at any time; CLIP simply computes their vector similarity on the fly.
Prompt templates convert raw labels into complete sentences, matching the caption format of the pre-training dataset.
exercises.sentence: To match CLIP's training context, class labels are typically wrapped in prompt ___ like 'a photo of a [class]'.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is a typical zero-shot prompt template for classifying satellite photos?
exercises.explain: Tailoring prompt templates to the target domain (satellite photos) helps CLIP extract more relevant vector representations.

options.text: 'a drawing of a [class] in a comic book'

exercises.prompt: Why does wrapping a class label in a template like 'a photo of a [class]' perform better than using the raw word (e.g. 'cat')?

options.text: Because CLIP was pre-trained on full sentences from the web; a single word is out-of-distribution and has weaker embedding representation.
Because single words contain too many characters.
Because it forces the model to ignore color channels.

exercises.explain: Spot on! Matching the sentence format CLIP saw during training guarantees cleaner, more reliable vector comparisons. Next: how CLIP is trained using contrastive loss!
